eli3: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training, Radford et al. 2021) learns to align images and text in a shared embedding space using contrastive learning. For 400 million (image, text) pairs scraped from the internet, CLIP trains an image encoder and a text encoder so that matching (image, caption) pairs have high cosine similarity and non-matching pairs have low similarity. After training, 'a photo of a dog' has a similar embedding to an image of a dog.

CLIP training: given a batch of N (image, text) pairs, compute N×N similarity matrix between all image and text embeddings. Contrastive loss (InfoNCE): maximize the N diagonal similarities (matched pairs) and minimize the N²-N off-diagonal similarities (mismatched pairs). Temperature parameter τ scales the logits. After training, the embedding space is shared — you can compare image and text embeddings directly with cosine similarity.

CLIP's key capability: zero-shot classification without any task-specific training. Given K class names, create K text prompts ('a photo of a {class}'), embed all prompts, embed the image, find the class with maximum cosine similarity. CLIP ViT-L/14 achieves 75.3% zero-shot accuracy on ImageNet — matching the original ResNet-50 trained on ImageNet labels, despite never seeing ImageNet during training. This launched the era of foundation vision models.

whenWhy.title: Zero-shot image classification
whenWhy.body: CLIP enables zero-shot classification for any set of categories you can describe in text — no labeled training images needed. Create text prompts for each class, embed all prompts and the image, argmax cosine similarity. Applications: content moderation (detect categories of harmful content), product classification (route items to categories by description), NSFW detection (text-described content policies). CLIP generalizes across domains unseen during training: medical images, satellite imagery, artwork.

whenWhy.title: Multimodal search and image retrieval
whenWhy.body: Since CLIP embeds images and text in the same space, you can search images with natural language queries. 'Find all images of people wearing red hats' → embed the query → cosine search over image embedding index. No image labeling required — images are indexed as embeddings. Used for: stock photo search (Getty, Adobe), product search (search by photo or description), content discovery. Also works in reverse: given an image, find text documents describing similar content.

whenWhy.title: Visual backbone for downstream VLMs
whenWhy.body: CLIP's visual encoder (ViT-L/14) has become the default backbone for VLMs (LLaVA, InstructBLIP, BLIP-2). Because CLIP's visual features are already aligned with text, they transfer better to language model spaces than features from a purely visual model (supervised ImageNet classification). The CLIP encoder is typically frozen when training VLMs — its rich text-aligned visual representations are a strong prior that reduces the visual instruction tuning data required.

deepDiveIntro: CLIP has two encoders that produce embeddings in the same shared space: an image encoder (a Vision Transformer or ResNet) and a text encoder (a GPT-2 style transformer limited to 77 tokens). The final image embedding comes from pooling over the patch embeddings; the final text embedding is taken from the last token position. Both are projected into a common embedding space where cosine similarity is meaningful across modalities.

The training loss is symmetric: for a batch of N image-text pairs, you compute the full N×N similarity matrix between all images and all texts, scaled by a temperature parameter. The loss pushes the N diagonal entries (matched pairs) to be high and all off-diagonal entries (mismatched pairs) to be low, doing this simultaneously for both the image-to-text and text-to-image directions. Larger batches mean more negatives per positive, which is why CLIP used a batch size of 32,768 across hundreds of GPUs.

deepDive.heading: Contrastive Training and InfoNCE Loss
deepDive.body: For each matched image-text pair in the batch, the loss treats all other texts as negatives for that image and all other images as negatives for that text. The temperature parameter scales how sharply the model must distinguish the correct pair from the rest — a lower temperature creates a harder, more discriminative task. Because the negatives come from the batch itself, larger batches provide harder and more diverse negatives, which is why scaling batch size so aggressively (32,768 pairs per step) was central to CLIP's success. [1]

deepDive.heading: Zero-Shot Classification Protocol
deepDive.body: To classify an image into K categories, you create a natural language prompt for each class name — 'a photo of a dog' rather than just 'dog' — and embed all prompts with the text encoder. Then embed the image and find the class prompt with the highest cosine similarity. The phrasing of the prompt matters: CLIP uses an ensemble of 80 differently worded templates and averages their embeddings, which consistently improves accuracy over any single template. Adding descriptive context ('a photo of a stingray, a type of fish') further helps for unusual classes. [1]

deepDive.heading: CLIP Variants and Successors
deepDive.body: OpenCLIP trained on the open LAION-2B dataset with a ViT-H/14 encoder outperforms the original CLIP on ImageNet zero-shot accuracy. SigLIP replaces the InfoNCE softmax loss with independent pairwise sigmoid comparisons — this removes the need to gather all negatives across devices, making it more memory-efficient at smaller batch sizes. CoCa adds a captioning decoder alongside the contrastive encoder, jointly training both objectives for stronger image-text representations. [1]

deepDive.heading: CLIP Limitations and Failure Modes
deepDive.body: CLIP treats text as a bag of words and misses relational structure: 'a dog bites a man' and 'a man bites a dog' produce nearly identical embeddings. It also struggles with counting, unusual attribute binding (confusing 'red car next to blue truck' with 'blue car next to red truck'), and specialised domains like medical imaging or satellite imagery that were underrepresented in training data. Text captions are capped at 77 tokens, so long descriptions are truncated. Models like LLaVA address these limitations by adding a language model on top of the CLIP encoder for richer reasoning. [1]

takeaways: CLIP: contrastive training on 400M (image, text) pairs — shared embedding space for image-text similarity. [1]
InfoNCE loss: maximize diagonal of N×N similarity matrix, large batch (32K) critical for quality. [1]
Zero-shot classification: 'a photo of a {class}' text prompts + cosine similarity — 75.3% ImageNet. [1]
SigLIP: sigmoid loss replaces softmax — more efficient, better at small batch sizes. [1]
Limitation: bag-of-words behavior — poor spatial/relational reasoning, counting, unusual attribute binding. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does CLIP use a separate text encoder rather than reusing a pre-trained language model?
edgeCases.a: CLIP trains both encoders from scratch jointly with the contrastive objective — this ensures both encode information in a shared, compatible space. Reusing a pre-trained LM (GPT-2) as the text encoder: the LM's embedding space is not aligned with visual features; the contrastive training would need to significantly shift it. CLIP's text encoder is much smaller than GPT-2 (transformer with 63M params vs 117M) — it only needs to produce a single embedding per caption, not generate text. Using a full LM is overpowered for this task and would slow training. Later models (CoCa, LLaVA) do use pre-trained LMs as the decoder for caption generation, not as the CLIP text encoder. [1]

edgeCases.q: How does CLIP compare to supervised ImageNet pre-training for transfer learning?
edgeCases.a: For standard visual tasks (ImageNet classification, COCO object detection), supervised ImageNet pre-training is still competitive or better than CLIP — supervised training on labeled data is highly optimized for the specific task. CLIP advantages: (1) Zero-shot transfer to novel categories without any labeled examples; (2) Better robustness to distribution shift (CLIP trained on diverse web images generalizes better to out-of-distribution benchmarks); (3) Natural language interface — fine-grained control over what features to extract via text prompts. Empirical finding (Radford et al. 2021): CLIP ViT-L/14 trained on 400M pairs achieves better robustness on ImageNet distribution shift benchmarks than ResNet-101 trained on ImageNet, despite similar in-distribution accuracy. [1]

sources.label: Radford et al. (2021) — Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP), ICML 2021
